Algorithms: etymology
Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi - Persian mathematician ; wrote the
ﬁrst book on algebra (about 830).
(cid:73) methods for number addition, multiplication and division.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
4 / 54

Algorithms: deﬁnition
(cid:73) There is no standard deﬁnition for the notion of algorithm.
(cid:73) Cambridge Dictionary:
“A set of mathematical instructions that must be followed in a ﬁxed
order, and that, especially if given to a computer, will help to
calculate an answer to a mathematical problem.”
(cid:73) Schneider and Gersting 1995 (Invitation for Computer Science):
“An algorithm is a well-ordered collection of unambiguous and
eﬀectively computable operations that when executed produces a
result and halts in a ﬁnite amount of time.”
(cid:73) Gersting and Schneider 2012 (Invitation for Computer Science, 6nd
edition):
“An algorithm is ordered sequence of instructions that is guaranteed
to solve a speciﬁc problem.”
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
5 / 54

Algorithms: deﬁnition
(cid:73) Wikipedia:
“In mathematics and computer science, an algorithm is a step-by-step
procedure for calculations. Algorithms are used for calculation, data
processing, and automated reasoning. An algorithm is an eﬀective
method expressed as a ﬁnite list of well-deﬁned instructions for
calculating a function. Starting from an initial state and initial input
(perhaps empty), the instructions describe a computation that, when
executed, proceeds through a ﬁnite number of well-deﬁned successive
states, eventually producing “output” and terminating at a ﬁnal
ending state. The transition from one state to the next is not
necessarily deterministic; some algorithms, known as randomized
algorithms, incorporate random input.”
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
6 / 54

Algorithms: computing model, solved problem
All deﬁnitions have something in common:
(cid:73) data / information and their processing in steps.
(cid:73) In general, this is described by a computing model.
A computing model is given by:
(cid:73) memory - data representation.
(cid:73) instructions
syntax - syntactic description of processing steps;
semantic - describes the processing steps when executing one
instruction; it is in general a transition relation between conﬁgurations
(transition system).
(cid:73) An algorithm has to produce a result, that is has to solve a problem.
(cid:73) A problem can be seen as a pair (input, output), where input
describes the input data (instance), while output describes the output
data (result).
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
7 / 54

Algorithms + Data Structures = Programs. — Niklaus Wirth
I will, in fact, claim that the diﬀerence between a bad programmer and a
good one is whether he considers his code or his data structures more
important. Bad programmers worry about the code. Good programmers
worry about data structures and their relationships. — Linus Torvalds
Algorithms and Data Structures
(cid:73) Algorithm: method to solve a problem.
(cid:73) Data structures: method to keep/represent data/information.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
8 / 54

Algorithms and Data Structures
(cid:73) Algorithm: method to solve a problem.
(cid:73) Data structures: method to keep/represent data/information.
Algorithms + Data Structures = Programs. — Niklaus Wirth
I will, in fact, claim that the diﬀerence between a bad programmer and a
good one is whether he considers his code or his data structures more
important. Bad programmers worry about the code. Good programmers
worry about data structures and their relationships. — Linus Torvalds
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
8 / 54

Algorithms: properties
(cid:73) input – zero or more supplied data entities.
(cid:73) output – the information produced by the algorithm.
(cid:73) termination – for any input, the algorithm executes a ﬁnite number
of steps.
(cid:73) correctness – the algorithm ends and produces the correct output for
any input instance; we say that the algorithm solves the given
problem.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
9 / 54

Algorithms: eﬃciency
(cid:73) An algorithm should use a reasonable amount of resources::
[space of] memory and [execution] time.
(cid:73) Eﬃcient algorithms are needed for:
(cid:73) save waiting times, storage space, energy consumption, etc.;
(cid:73) scalability: solving problems of higher dimensions using the same
resources (CPU, memory, disk, etc.);
(cid:73) optimized solution.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
10 / 54

FII, UAIC

Algorithm description
(cid:73) informal: natural language.
(cid:73) formal:
(cid:73) mathematical notation (Turing machine, lambda-calculus (Church),
recursive functions, etc.);
(cid:73) programming languages: high level, low level, declarative (i.e.,
functional programming, logic programming). This can be also an
informal model if there is no a formal semantic of the language.
(cid:73) semi-formal:
(cid:73) pseudo-code: combines the formal notation of a programming language
with natural language;
(cid:73) graphical notation: logic schemes, state machines, activity diagrams.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
13 / 54

Content
Algorithms. Introduction
Algorithmic language
Data types
Arrays and structures
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
14 / 54

Algorithmic language
There is a need for a language that is
(cid:73) simple: easy to understand;
(cid:73) expressive: in order to describe algorithms;
(cid:73) abstract: when describing an algorithm the focus should be on the
algorithmic thinking and not on the implementation details;
A computing models that is appropriate for complexity analysis, in
particular the time complexity.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
15 / 54

(cid:73) Address

Instructions
(cid:73) Expressions
(cid:73) Block: {instructions}
(cid:73) Conditional: if if-else
(cid:73) Iterative: while repeat for
(cid:73) sequence break: return
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
23 / 54

Instructions
(cid:73) Assignment
(cid:73) Syntax: < variable >←< expression >
(cid:73) Semantic:
(cid:73) < expression > is evaluated and the results is stored in the memory
location associated with < variable >;
(cid:73) it is the only instruction that can modify the memory content.
(cid:73) uniform cost O(1), logarithmic O(log < expression >)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
24 / 54

Assignment
Example:
(cid:73) Before assignment :
(cid:73) After assignment u ← −v ∗ u:
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
25 / 54

Instructions
(cid:73) Pointer assignment
(cid:73) Syntax:
∗ < pointer variable >←< expression >
(cid:73) Semantic:
(cid:73) < expression > is evaluated and the result is stored in the memory
location that has the address < pointer variable >
(cid:73) Example: ∗p ← 10
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
26 / 54

Instructions
(cid:73) if
(cid:73) Syntax:
if < expression > then
< instruction − sequence1 >
else
< instruction − sequence2 >
if < expression > then
< instruction − sequence1 >
Remark: the < expression > evaluation yields a Boolean result.
(cid:73) Semantic:
(cid:73) < expression > is evaluated. If the result is true, then the
< instruction − sequence1 > is executed, and if the result is false, then
the < instruction − sequence2 > is executed; after-that the execution
of the if instruction is terminated.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
27 / 54

Instructions: if
(cid:73) uniform cost O(1), logarithmic cost O(1)
(cid:73) Example: computing minimum of two numbers a and b:
if a < b then
min ← a
else
min ← b
or
min ← a
if b < a then
min ← b
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
28 / 54

Instructions: while
(cid:73) while
(cid:73) Syntax:
while < expression > do
< instruction − sequence >
(cid:73) Semantic:
1. < expression > is evaluated
2.
If the result is true then < instruction − sequence > is executed and
the cycle is repeated from step 1. If the result is false then the
execution of while is terminated.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
29 / 54

while example
(cid:73) smallest k such that 7k >= n for a given n
k ← 0
seven power k ← 1
while seven power k < n do
k ← k + 1
seven power k ← seven power k ∗ 7
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
30 / 54

Instructions: repeat
(cid:73) repeat
(cid:73) Syntax:
repeat
< instruction − sequence >
until < expression >;
(cid:73) Semantic:
Instruction:
repeat
S
until e;
simulates the execution of the following algorithm:
S
while not e do
S
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
31 / 54

repeat: example
(cid:73) smallest k such that 7k >= n for a given n
k ← 0
seven power k ← 1
repeat
k ← k + 1
seven power k ← seven power k ∗ 7
until seven power k >= n;
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
32 / 54

Instructions: for
(cid:73) for
(cid:73) Syntax:
for < variable >←< expression1 > to < expression2 > do
< instruction − sequence >
or
for < variable >←< expression1 > downto < expression2 > do
< instruction − sequence >
Remark: < variable > is an integer variable, and < expression1 > and
< expression2 > evaluates to an integer
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
33 / 54

Instructions: for
(cid:73) for
(cid:73) Semantic:
for i ← e1 to e2 do
S
is equivalent to:
i ← e1
temp ← e2
while i <= temp do
S
i ← i + 1
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
34 / 54

Instructions: for
(cid:73) for
(cid:73) Semantic:
for i ← e1 downto e2 do
S
is equivalent to:
i ← e1
temp ← e2
while i >= temp do
S
i ← i − 1
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
35 / 54

Subprograms
(cid:73) The language is modular: a program contains a number of modules.
(cid:73) One module is identiﬁed by a subprogram.
(cid:73) Subprograms:
(cid:73) Procedures
(cid:73) Functions
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
36 / 54

Subprograms
(cid:73) Procedures:
(cid:73) Syntax:
Procedure name (formal-parameter-list)
begin
instruction sequence
end
(cid:73) Call: NAME(actual-parameter-list)
(cid:73) The interface between a procedure and a calling module is made
through the parameters and global variables.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
37 / 54

Procedures
(cid:73) Example:
Procedure SWAP (x,y)
begin
aux ← x
x ← y
y ← aux
end
Call:
SWAP(a, b)
SWAP(b, c)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
38 / 54

Subprograms
(cid:73) Functions:
(cid:73) Syntax:
Function name (formal-parameter-list)
begin
instruction-sequence
end
instruction sequence contains at least one return < expr > instruction.
(cid:73) Call: NAME(actual-parameter-list)
used within an expression: the returned value is the one obtained by
evaluating < expr >.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
39 / 54

Functions
(cid:73) Example:
Function max3(x,y,z)
begin
temp ← x
if y > temp then
temp ← y
if z > temp then
temp ← z
return temp
end
Call:
max3(a, b, c)
2*max3(a, b, c) > 5
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
40 / 54

Content
Algorithms. Introduction
Algorithmic language
Data types
Arrays and structures
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
41 / 54

Arrays
(cid:73) Homogeneous collection of variables called array components
(cid:73) All components belong to the same type
(cid:73) Components are identiﬁed through indices
(cid:73) Arrays can be used to sets, sequences (the order of elements is
important), matrices.
(cid:73) Arrays can be:
(cid:73) one-dimension (vectors)
(cid:73) bi-dimensional (matrices)
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
42 / 54

uni-dimensional unidimensionale
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
43 / 54

Uni-dimensional arrays
(cid:73) The memory is a contiguous sequence of locations.
(cid:73) The storage order – indices order
(cid:73) Operations are at component level
Example:
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
a[i] ← 0
for i ← 0 to n − 1 do
c[i] ← a[i] + b[i]
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
44 / 54

Arrays
Operation cost:
operation
a[i]
a[i] ← v
time
uniform cost
O(1)
O(1)
logarithmic cost
O(i + logai )
O(i + logv )
where a is a n–element array, with components a[0], ..., a[n − 1]
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
45 / 54

Bi-dimensional arrays
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
46 / 54

Bi-dimensional arrays
(cid:73) Contiguous memory of m × n locations
(cid:73) Components are identiﬁed by two indices:
(cid:73) ﬁrst index ranges {0, 1, . . . , m − 1}
(cid:73) second index ranges {0, 1, . . . , n − 1}
(cid:73) component variables :
a[0, 0], a[0, 1], . . . , a[0, n − 1], a[1, 0], a[1, 1], . . . , a[1, n − 1], . . . , a[m −
1, 0], a[m − 1, 1], . . . , a[m − 1, n − 1]
(cid:73) The storage order follows the lexicographic order of indices.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
47 / 54

Bi-dimensional arrays
(cid:73) A bi-dimensional array can be also seen as an one dimensional array
for which the components are one-dimensional arrays.
(cid:73) Notation: a[0][0], a[0][1], . . . , a[0][n − 1], . . . ,
a[m − 1][0], a[m − 1][1], . . . , a[m − 1][n − 1]
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
48 / 54

Bi-dimensional arrays
(cid:73) Operations are at component level:
for i ← 0 to m − 1 do
for j ← 0 to n–1 do
c[i, j] ← 0
for k ← 0 to p–1 do
c[i, j] ← c[i, j] + a[i, k] ∗ b[k, j]
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
49 / 54

Strings
(cid:73) They can be looked as uni-dimensional arrays with elements of type
char.
(cid:73) String constants are denoted by “ ”: “string”
(cid:73) Operations: Concatenation denoted by +:
“a string” + “another string” = “a stringanother string”
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
50 / 54

Structures
(cid:73) Structure: Heterogeneous collection of variables called ﬁelds.
(cid:73) A structure has a name and each ﬁeld has its own name and type.
(cid:73) Examples:
1. a point in a plan can be represented by a structure “point” with two
ﬁelds: x and y ;
2. One person can be represented by a structure “person” with three
ﬁelds: name, age, address;
(cid:73) Specifying a ﬁeld:
point.x, point.y
person.name, person.age, person.address.street
if p is pointer to a person structure then: p− > age
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
51 / 54

Structures
(cid:73) The allocated memory to a structure is a contiguous one; ﬁelds are
stored in the order of deﬁnition inside the structure.
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
DS 2018/2019
52 / 54

Structures
Operation cost:
operation
S.x
S.x ← v
time
uniform cost
O(1)
O(1)
logarithmic cost
O(logSx )
O(logv )
FII, UAIC
Lecture 1
